metadata.title: Why a Barrier Can Suddenly Turn Invisible
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one accent. TEAL = resonant transmission / transparent barrier; CRIMSON = reflected wave / partial bounce
metadata.greeting_note: hello lexicon: Thai. Medhavy 1-word budget. Wagwan is Bear-only.
metadata.audio_note: All existing inner-beat mp3s were generated with ElevenLabs NBB voice. Must regenerate ALL with kokoro af_kore for Medhavy: python3 runtime/scripts/generate_audio.py quantum-mechanics-vol1/youtube/vox-ramsauer-resonance

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. A normal barrier always reflects some wave, so transmission never reaches one hundred percent. At a special energy the barrier becomes perfectly transparent and the reflected wave vanishes completely. Inside the well the wavelength shortens, and the front and back walls each reflect part of the wave. When the width is a whole number of half-wavelengths, the two reflected waves cancel; two k-two L equals n pi.

artifactLines: A normal barrier always reflects some wave, so transmission never reaches one hundred percent.
At a special energy the barrier becomes perfectly transparent and the reflected wave vanishes completely.
Inside the well the wavelength shortens, and the front and back walls each reflect part of the wave.
When the width is a whole number of half-wavelengths, the two reflected waves cancel; two k-two L equals n pi.

beats.narration_text: So perfect transparency happens when the two wall reflections are exactly out of phase and cancel, exactly like a lens coating. I want to map the full transmission curve. Claude, please (1) plot transmission versus energy for the 0.3 nanometer, 5 eV barrier and mark the resonance peaks; (2) show how the spacing of those peaks depends on the barrier width; (3) explain why the transmission dips but never quite reaches zero between resonances.

props.segment: Why a Barrier Can Suddenly Turn Invisible
props.command: So perfect transparency happens when the two wall reflections are exactly out of phase and cancel, exactly like a lens coating. I want to map the full transmission curve. Claude, please (1) plot transmission versus energy for the 0.3 nanometer, 5 eV barrier and mark the resonance peaks; (2) show how the spacing of those peaks depends on the barrier width; (3) explain why the transmission dips but never quite reaches zero between resonances.

beats.narration_text: Tune a particle's energy just right and a solid barrier becomes perfectly transparent — the particle sails through as if it weren't there. Change the energy slightly and it bounces back.

card.copy: Why a Barrier Can Suddenly Turn Invisible

props.segment: Why a Barrier Can Suddenly Turn Invisible
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: a wave hitting a barrier — show partial transmission (TEAL) and partial reflection (CRIMSON), with the transmission never reaching 100 percent, label T < 1"

beats.narration_text: A standard barrier: some wave tunnels through, some reflects. The transmission never reaches one hundred percent. The reflected wave is always there.
At a special energy, the barrier becomes perfectly transparent. Not ninety-nine percent — exactly one hundred percent passes through. The reflected wave vanishes completely. Why?

card.copy: Normal barrier: always some reflection. At one special energy: zero reflection, perfect transmission. How does a solid barrier turn invisible?

props.segment: Why a Barrier Can Suddenly Turn Invisible
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: a wave packet entering a square well barrier — show the wavelength shortening inside the well (higher kinetic energy), with the wave bouncing back and forth between the two walls"

beats.narration_text: Inside the barrier, the wave has a shorter wavelength — the particle has more kinetic energy because of the well depth. The wave bounces back and forth from the two walls.

props.segment: Why a Barrier Can Suddenly Turn Invisible
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: two reflected waves from the front and back walls of a barrier — show them traveling back toward the incoming beam, with arrows indicating their directions"

beats.narration_text: The front wall reflects some wave back. The back wall also reflects some wave back. When those two reflected waves travel back to the front, they can interfere with each other.

props.segment: Why a Barrier Can Suddenly Turn Invisible
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: two reflected waves arriving at the barrier entrance exactly out of phase — show them canceling by destructive interference, leaving zero net reflection"

beats.narration_text: When the barrier width is exactly a whole number of half-wavelengths, the two reflected waves are exactly out of phase with each other — they cancel by destructive interference. The reflected wave vanishes.

props.segment: Why a Barrier Can Suddenly Turn Invisible
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: anti-reflection coating on a lens — thin film of correct thickness makes two reflected beams cancel, label the film thickness as lambda/4 — compare to barrier resonance at lambda/2"

beats.narration_text: This is the same trick as an anti-reflection coating on a camera lens — a thin film of just the right thickness makes the two reflected beams cancel. The physics is identical, only the wavelength scale differs.

props.segment: Why a Barrier Can Suddenly Turn Invisible
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: plot transmission T vs incoming energy for a square well barrier — show a sharp peak at resonance (T=1), flanked by partial transmission, label the resonance condition 2*k2*L = n*pi"

beats.narration_text: Resonance condition: two k-two times L equals n times pi. The wavelength inside times the width equals an integer number of half-wavelengths. Satisfied — zero reflection. Off by even a little — reflection jumps back.

props.segment: Why a Barrier Can Suddenly Turn Invisible
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: illustrative case — 0.3 nm barrier width, 5 eV depth, plot T vs electron kinetic energy showing first resonance peak at ~3 eV where wavelength inside is 0.6 nm (one half-wavelength)"

beats.narration_text: Illustrative example: a barrier of width 0.3 nanometers and depth 5 eV. An electron at about 3 eV kinetic energy hits the first resonance — the wavelength inside is 0.6 nanometers, the barrier is exactly one half-wavelength wide. Perfect transmission.
The barrier doesn't disappear. It fights itself. At the right width and energy, the reflections from both walls cancel each other — and everything gets through.

card.copy: The barrier fights itself. Reflections from both walls cancel. At the right energy, everything gets through.

beats.narration_text: At resonance, the barrier disappears. Paste this into Claude and find where the transmission peaks.

props.segment: Why a Barrier Can Suddenly Turn Invisible
props.command: claude "simulate the Ramsauer-Townsend effect: plot transmission probability vs electron energy for a square-well potential, showing the resonance peaks where the well becomes transparent"
